title: Cost of Inaction — Loss Model
READ_THIS_FIRST: This is a MODEL, not a measurement. Every number below is either a sourced external statistic (tagged) or an assumption the authors invented (tagged). Its purpose is to make the loss structure explicit and attackable — not to prove a market. If you present this as evidence of demand, you will be caught, because the assumptions are visible and several of them are shaky. The correct use is: 'here is our model, here are the assumptions, tell us which one you think is wrong.' That framing wins meetings. 'Our research shows PHP X million' loses them. This revision extends the model across all three phases of the platform: the original categories below (catastrophic mortality, feed waste, aeration, disease, and so on) sit at the Phase 3 hardware tier and carry whatever external evidence they always did. The new categories added at the end of this list — beginner attrition, scaling blindness, expert-access gap, and market inefficiency — sit at the Phase 1/2 software tiers and carry NO external evidence at all, because no incident-reporting mechanism exists for them. They are labelled 'assumption' or 'modeled_projection' precisely because that is all they are, and they should be read, and attacked, accordingly.

loss_categories.label: Catastrophic Mortality Events
loss_categories.description: Mass fish kills. Sudden, total or near-total loss of standing stock in affected units.

anchors.fact: 343 tonnes lost in a single Taal Lake event
Over 600 tonnes / PHP 43.13 million in a single tilapia kill
Up to PHP 15 million lost by Pangasinan bangus operators in one event
PHP 322.1 million estimated recorded national fish kill losses 1998-2018

evidence.the_contradiction_we_must_resolve: Three single events in this list total roughly PHP 58 million plus 343 tonnes. The twenty-year national recorded total is PHP 322 million. These cannot be reconciled unless the national figure counts only a narrow subset of officially investigated incidents. Either the national figure is a severe undercount, or the event figures are overstated by reporting. We do not know which, and until we do, neither number should anchor a business case. Resolving this by obtaining BFAR's primary records is a Phase 0 task and a genuinely publishable contribution.

loss_categories.honest_addressability: We can shorten detection time. We cannot make a farmer destock, and we cannot manufacture emergency aeration capacity that does not exist. On a farm with aerators and a manager willing to run them, an early warning has real value. On a smallholder cage with neither, the warning converts an unexpected loss into an expected one — which is worth something psychologically and nothing financially. The addressable fraction is therefore a function of the customer's response capacity, not of our detection performance. This is the most important sentence in this file.

loss_categories.label: Chronic Sub-Lethal Production Loss
loss_categories.description: Fish that survive but grow slowly, convert feed poorly, and die at elevated background rates because water quality sits chronically at the margin.
loss_categories.visibility: near zero — nobody reports this because nobody measures it
loss_categories.hypothesis: This category is larger in aggregate than catastrophic mortality, possibly by an order of magnitude, and it is completely invisible in every published loss statistic.
loss_categories.reasoning: A kill event is a discrete, reported, dramatic loss. A 12 percent reduction in growth rate across an entire cycle across an entire sector is silent, is absorbed into the expected yield, and is never attributed to anything. Farmers do not experience it as a loss; they experience it as normal.
loss_categories.why_this_matters_enormously: If this hypothesis is true, the OmniDrone's value proposition is misstated throughout this repository. We are pitching catastrophe insurance, when the real product might be continuous yield optimisation — a different message, a different customer conversation, a different pricing model, and a far better business, because it pays on every cycle rather than on the rare bad one. Catastrophe is a more emotionally compelling pitch; chronic loss may be the more valuable and more defensible one.
loss_categories.how_to_test_it: Phase 3's longitudinal deployment can measure it directly: correlate continuous water quality against realised FCR and survival across a full cycle, on farms with no kill events. If the correlation is strong, restructure the whole pitch around it.
loss_categories.addressable_by_omnidrone: strongly — this is exactly what continuous profiling is for
loss_categories.intellectual_note: This is the most important untested claim in the entire repository. It is flagged as speculative because it is, but it deserves the team's attention far more than another decimal place on the mortality figures.

loss_categories.label: Feed Waste
loss_categories.description: Feed dispensed that is not converted to biomass — uneaten, or eaten by fish too stressed to convert it.
loss_categories.visibility: low — recorded as cost, never as waste

anchors.fact: Feed is approximately 60 percent of total operating cost (NOAA, cited via Innovasea); other sources put it as high as 70 percent, and 50-65 percent of variable cost in prawn culture
At a feed price of USD 500/t, a 0.1 difference in FCR is worth USD 50 per tonne of production
FCR typically ranges 1.0-2.5 in finfish culture

the_leverage.statement: Because feed is ~60 percent of operating cost, and because the USD 50/tonne figure for a 0.1 FCR improvement is externally sourced, feed is the one loss category where the value of an improvement can be computed from published numbers rather than invented. That makes it the ROI argument to lead with — it is the only one that does not require the customer to accept our assumptions.

worked_example.scenario: A farm producing 100 tonnes per cycle, feed at USD 500/t.
worked_example.caveat: Whether the OmniDrone actually delivers a 0.1 FCR improvement is completely unproven. The arithmetic is sound; the premise is a hypothesis. State it that way.

loss_categories.addressable_by_omnidrone: plausibly — via condition-aware feeding and even distribution
loss_categories.honest_note: We do not observe feeding response directly. An underwater camera or a hydroacoustic sensor sees whether the fish are actually eating; we infer from water condition and distribution. That is a weaker feedback loop than the incumbent underwater feeding systems provide, and a knowledgeable buyer will say so.

loss_categories.label: Self-Inflicted Water Degradation
loss_categories.description: The uneaten feed from the category above decomposes, consumes oxygen, and releases ammonia — degrading the water that then causes the losses in the categories above.
loss_categories.the_structural_insight: This is a feedback loop, and it is the most intellectually interesting thing in the problem space. Overfeeding costs money AND degrades water AND therefore increases mortality AND stresses fish so they convert feed worse AND therefore requires more feed. The loop is vicious and it closes.
loss_categories.why_it_matters_for_the_pitch: It means a single intervention — feeding on evidence rather than on schedule — attacks the largest cost line and the primary kill mechanism simultaneously. That is not a feature list; that is a coherent thesis about the system's dynamics, and it is what elevates this from 'a drone with sensors' to a considered argument. Put this on a slide as a loop diagram. It is the best slide available to this company.

loss_categories.label: Disease-Related Losses

anchors.fact: FAO estimates global aquaculture disease losses at approximately USD 6 billion annually
WSSV losses in Asia estimated at USD 6 billion during the 1992-93 outbreaks and USD 1-2 billion annually thereafter
EMS/AHPND in the Mekong Delta costs small-scale producers roughly USD 800 million per year

loss_categories.brutal_honesty: Almost none of this is ours. Disease losses are driven by pathogen introduction, broodstock quality, and biosecurity — not by observability. We detect the consequences earlier and we flag stress windows. Those are real but modest contributions. This category belongs in the problem picture because it sizes the sector's pain; it does NOT belong in our revenue model, and putting it there would be the single most dishonest move available in this pitch. Resist it.

loss_categories.label: Aeration Energy Waste
loss_categories.description: Aerators run continuously because nobody knows when they are needed, or run too late because nobody knew they were.
loss_categories.visibility: high as a cost, invisible as a waste — it is just 'the electricity bill'
loss_categories.why_this_is_the_sleeper: This is the easiest ROI in the entire product and it is buried at position six in this file. Aeration electricity is a large, monthly, already-metered cost. A pilot that shows a farm ran its aerators 30 percent fewer hours with equal or better DO outcomes is a complete, self-contained, verifiable business case that requires no belief in our mortality claims at all. It can be demonstrated in one cycle. It should probably be the lead ROI in the first commercial pitch, precisely because it is boring and checkable.
loss_categories.caveat: The 30 percent figure above is illustrative and unmeasured. The point is the structure of the argument, not the number.
loss_categories.data_needed: Baseline aeration hours and kWh from a real farm. This is obtainable in Phase 0 by asking. Do it.

loss_categories.label: Monitoring Labour and Occupational Risk
loss_categories.note: The monetary value is modest — a technician's hours. The human value is not. Pre-dawn small-boat operation in monsoon weather is a genuine drowning risk that the industry treats as routine. This category should never be reduced to a labour cost line; it is an argument that stands on its own and it lands with cooperatives, LGUs, and development funders in a way that FCR arithmetic does not.

loss_categories.label: The Cost of Never Learning
loss_categories.description: Fifteen years of operation producing zero retained knowledge about the farm's own water.
loss_categories.note: Unquantifiable and real. Every cycle begins with the same ignorance as the first. The sector cannot build the predictive models that would help it because the training data was never kept. This is the argument for the platform as infrastructure rather than as a gadget — and it is the argument that justifies a subscription rather than a hardware sale, because the value compounds with duration.

loss_categories.label: Beginner Attrition and Preventable First-Cycle Loss (Phase 1 tier)
loss_categories.description: First-time and smallholder farmers who lose money, or give up entirely, because of mistakes a structured onboarding, basic logging habit, or accessible disease screening would have caught — over-stocking a pond past its carrying capacity, missing an early disease sign, or simply not knowing what a healthy cycle is supposed to look like.
loss_categories.visibility: near zero — nobody surveys farmer attrition, and a farmer who quietly stops farming does not file a report with anyone
loss_categories.hypothesis: Given that small-scale producers make up the large majority of aquaculture operators globally, and that extension services are thin and unevenly distributed, it is plausible that preventable beginner-tier losses and dropouts outnumber every catastrophic mortality event in this repository combined, simply on population size. This is a hypothesis about scale, not a measurement of it.
loss_categories.reasoning: fao-sofia-2024 establishes that small-scale and inland producers dominate global aquaculture by headcount; it does NOT establish an attrition rate, a loss magnitude, or anything about the Philippines specifically. The citation supports only the premise that this population is large, not the size of its losses.
loss_categories.why_this_matters_enormously: If beginner attrition is the sector's largest source of preventable loss by sheer volume of affected people, then Phase 1's free app is not merely a funnel for Phase 2 and 3 — it may be addressing the single largest problem in this entire repository, just one that is individually small, unreported, and invisible to every other loss category here. This is exactly the kind of claim that must not be presented as established, because it currently rests on population statistics and inference, not on any measured attrition data.
loss_categories.how_to_test_it: Phase 0/1 discovery: track how many first-time users of the free app abandon farming within their first cycle, and ask departing users why, however informally that can be done before there is a real user base to study.
loss_categories.addressable_by_omnidrone: not applicable — this is a Phase 1 software category, addressed by the free app's guidance, logging, disease-ID, and community features, not by hardware
loss_categories.intellectual_note: This is the Phase 1 analogue of the chronic-sublethal hypothesis above: both claim that the quiet, unreported version of a problem outweighs the dramatic, reported version. Neither claim has been tested. Both should be treated with equal suspicion and equal interest.

loss_categories.label: Scaling Blindness and Missed Optimisation (Phase 2 tier)
loss_categories.description: Farms that have grown past a size a notebook or spreadsheet can meaningfully track, and therefore miss cross-unit comparisons, early trend signals, and the compounding value of expert input, because no analytics layer exists between 'too big to track by hand' and 'big enough to justify Phase 3 hardware.'
loss_categories.visibility: invisible — shows up only as an unexplained gap between a farm's best and worst cycles, never attributed to the absence of a tool
loss_categories.hypothesis: A farm operating a dozen units with no cross-unit analytics is very likely leaving some avoidable underperformance on the table every cycle, in the same reflexive way that unmeasured feed waste degrades water quality elsewhere in this file. No magnitude is claimed here because none has been measured.
loss_categories.addressable_by_omnidrone: not applicable — this is the Phase 2 premium analytics and predictive-health category
loss_categories.note: This category and 'expert-access-gap' below are two faces of the same underlying gap: a growing farm has neither the internal analytical capacity nor the affordable external expertise to catch what a notebook cannot show it. Splitting them into two entries reflects that they are addressed by two different Phase 2 features (analytics dashboards versus paid consultations), not that they are two different problems.
loss_categories.how_to_test_it: Requires real Phase 2 users with logged history over multiple cycles — cannot be tested before Phase 1 has produced that data. This is a multi-year dependency, not a Phase 0 question.

loss_categories.label: Cost of No Affordable Expert Access (Phase 2 tier)
loss_categories.description: Decisions a farm manager would make differently with an aquaculturist's or vet's input, made instead alone, because retaining expertise directly is not justified at this scale and finding it on short notice is hard.
loss_categories.visibility: invisible — shows up as a decision that was 'probably fine' rather than as a counted loss
loss_categories.addressable_by_omnidrone: not applicable — this is the Phase 2 paid-consultation marketplace category
loss_categories.honest_note: No attempt is made here to price this gap. Doing so would require knowing how often a consultation would have changed the outcome and by how much, which is unknown and would be invented if stated as a number.

loss_categories.label: Cost of Informal, Opaque Input and Output Markets (Phase 2 tier)
loss_categories.description: Value lost to price opacity and weak negotiating position when farms buy feed and equipment or sell harvest through personal networks and informal middlemen rather than a transparent marketplace.
loss_categories.visibility: invisible — absorbed into 'that is just the price' on both sides of the transaction
loss_categories.addressable_by_omnidrone: not applicable — this is the Phase 2 marketplace category, and also the source of Phase 2's take-rate revenue line
loss_categories.honest_note: This category doubles as a revenue opportunity for the company (marketplace take-rate) and a claimed benefit to the farmer (better prices). Those two framings are in some tension — a take-rate is itself a cost extracted from the transaction — and the pitch should not claim we make the market free of friction when what we actually do is replace informal friction with a transparent, priced one. State it that way rather than papering over it.

model_assumptions_register.purpose: Every assumption a reader might otherwise have to reverse-engineer. Attack these.

assumptions.assumption: Earlier detection translates into avoided loss.
assumptions.status: UNTESTED — and it is the load-bearing assumption of the entire venture. If it is false, nothing else matters.
assumptions.how_to_test: Find or create a matched comparison: farms with monitoring vs without, same lake, same season.

assumptions.assumption: Farms have the response capacity to act on a warning.
assumptions.status: UNTESTED and probably false for smallholders.
assumptions.how_to_test: Ask, in Phase 0. This is a ten-minute question and it is currently unanswered.

assumptions.assumption: Chronic sub-lethal loss exceeds catastrophic loss in aggregate.
assumptions.status: SPECULATIVE. Potentially the most valuable hypothesis in the repository.

assumptions.assumption: Published fish kill statistics undercount true losses.
assumptions.status: PLAUSIBLE given the internal contradiction documented above, but unverified.

assumptions.assumption: Feed cost at ~60 percent of operating cost holds for Philippine tilapia and milkfish specifically.
assumptions.status: The 60 percent figure is sourced to NOAA via a US-oriented publication. It may not transfer to Philippine species and practices.
assumptions.how_to_test: Local farm cost structure data. Do not assume US figures transfer.

assumptions.assumption: A 0.1 FCR improvement is achievable through better water management.
assumptions.status: UNPROVEN. Plausible from the physiology — stressed fish convert feed poorly — but we have no evidence of the magnitude.

assumptions.assumption: Beginner-tier attrition and preventable first-cycle loss is large in aggregate, potentially exceeding catastrophic mortality by headcount of affected people.
assumptions.status: SPECULATIVE. Rests on population statistics (small-scale producers dominate the sector) plus inference, not on any measured attrition or dropout rate.
assumptions.how_to_test: Instrument the free app from day one to track first-cycle abandonment and ask departing users why. Cannot be tested until there are real users.

assumptions.assumption: A meaningful fraction of free Phase 1 users convert into paying Phase 2 customers.
assumptions.status: UNTESTED and load-bearing for the entire phased revenue model. If the conversion rate is near zero, Phase 1 is a cost centre with no funnel beneath it.
assumptions.how_to_test: Cannot be tested before Phase 1 has a real user base and enough elapsed time for farms to grow. This is a multi-cycle, multi-year question, not a Phase 0 one.

assumptions.assumption: Growing farms without analytics or predictive tools are leaving meaningful, avoidable performance on the table (scaling blindness).
assumptions.status: SPECULATIVE. Plausible by analogy to the feed-waste reflexivity argument, but no magnitude has been estimated and none should be invented.
assumptions.how_to_test: Requires Phase 2 users with multi-cycle logged history to compare against farms without analytics access.

assumptions.assumption: Farmers will pay for expert consultation and marketplace access at a price that supports a viable take-rate business.
assumptions.status: UNTESTED. No pricing signal exists yet from any real farmer.
assumptions.how_to_test: Phase 0/2 discovery: ask growing-farm managers directly what they currently pay informal consultants or middlemen, and whether a transparent alternative would command a premium or a discount.

what_this_file_deliberately_does_not_con.statement: There is no bottom-line 'total addressable loss' number here, and its absence is intentional. This applies across all three phases: there is no single peso or dollar figure anywhere in this file that purports to size the beginner-attrition, scaling-blindness, expert-access, or market-inefficiency categories, because none of the inputs needed to compute one exist yet.
what_this_file_deliberately_does_not_con.why: Constructing one would require multiplying our sourced figures by our invented assumptions, producing a large, authoritative-looking number whose error bars span an order of magnitude. That number would then be repeated in the deck, in the thesis, and eventually back to us by an investor who had checked it. The temptation to produce it is strong and it should be resisted until the assumptions in the register above have been tested against real farms — and, for the Phase 1/2 categories, until there are real users to test them against at all. When they have been, this file becomes genuinely powerful. Until then, the honest deliverable is the loss STRUCTURE, not a loss total.

chart_hint.rendering_note: Render as a qualitative structure — visibility on one axis, addressability on the other — NOT as a pie chart of peso values. A pie chart implies we know the proportions. We do not.
